text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
Restaging Examination

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Mr. Marcus Delgado is a 58-year-old Hispanic male with known Stage IV adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases (KRAS G12D mutant, MSS). He completed 12 cycles of induction FOLFOX plus bevacizumab (with oxaliplatin discontinued at cycle 10 due to cumulative grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy) and has since been on maintenance 5-fluorouracil/leucovorin/bevacizumab for approximately 4 months. Examination performed for interval restaging assessment. Comparison: Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis approximately 4 months ago, at the conclusion of induction chemotherapy.

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the administration of 100 mL of Omnipaque 350 intravenous contrast with standard power injection. Axial acquisitions were obtained during the portal venous phase for the abdomen and pelvis, and during the pulmonary arterial/venous phase for the chest. Coronal and sagittal multiplanar reformations were generated and reviewed. Lung windows, soft tissue windows, and bone windows reviewed. Oral contrast was also administered. No acute adverse reaction to contrast.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
Two new pulmonary nodules are identified that were not present on the prior examination and are of significant concern.

— A rounded soft tissue density nodule measuring 1.1 cm in greatest axial dimension is present in the right lower lobe, situated in the posterior basal segment. The nodule has smooth, well-defined margins and soft tissue attenuation without calcification, cavitation, or satellite lesions. This nodule is new from the prior scan.

— A second nodule measuring 0.7 cm in greatest axial dimension is present in the right upper lobe, posterior segment. This lesion is similarly rounded, non-calcified, and without spiculation. This lesion is also new from the prior examination.

No additional pulmonary nodules are identified. No ground-glass opacities, consolidation, pleural effusion, or pneumothorax. The central airways are patent. No endobronchial lesions. The visualized bronchi are unremarkable bilaterally.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The heart is normal in size without pericardial effusion. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy above the threshold for pathologic significance. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber. No paratracheal, subcarinal, or axillary lymph node enlargement. Thyroid is not fully included in the field of view.

Pleura:
No pleural effusions. No pleural thickening or pleural masses.

Chest Wall and Bony Thorax:
No acute osseous abnormality. No lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for metastatic involvement of the ribs, sternum, thoracic spine, or clavicles within the field of view.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size and contour. Four previously identified hepatic metastases are again seen, with the following interval changes compared to the prior examination:

— Segment VI: The dominant lesion in the posterior right lobe (segment VI) measures 2.5 cm in greatest axial dimension, representing a slight increase from 2.3 cm on the prior study. The lesion demonstrates peripheral arterial enhancement with heterogeneous internal hypoattenuation on portal venous phase, consistent with a hypovascular metastasis with central necrosis/hypovascularity. This marginal increase, while modest, represents a change from the trend of shrinkage seen during induction therapy.

— Segment IVa: The lesion in the medial left lobe (segment IVa) measures approximately 1.2 cm, stable compared to the prior examination.

— Segment II: The small lesion in the lateral left lobe (segment II) measures 0.7 cm, unchanged.

— Segment VII: A previously described small lesion in segment VII now measures approximately 0.5 cm, slightly increased from 0.4 cm on prior imaging — this small change approaches the limits of measurement reproducibility but is noted.

No new hepatic lesions are identified. No biliary ductal dilation. The portal vein and hepatic veins are patent without thrombosis. No perihepatic fluid.

Gallbladder and Biliary System:
The gallbladder is present, without stones, wall thickening, or pericholecystic fluid. The common bile duct measures 4 mm, within normal limits.

Pancreas:
The pancreatic parenchyma is homogeneous. No pancreatic ductal dilation. No discrete pancreatic mass. No peripancreatic adenopathy or stranding.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size, measuring approximately 11 cm in craniocaudal dimension. No focal splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral adrenal glands are morphologically normal. No adrenal nodules or masses identified.

Kidneys and Ureters:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and parenchymal thickness. No hydronephrosis. No nephrolithiasis. No renal masses. Normal bilateral nephrogram. Ureters not dilated.

Retroperitoneal and Mesenteric Lymph Nodes:
No significant interval change in retroperitoneal or mesenteric lymphadenopathy. No clearly enlarged lymph nodes exceeding 1 cm in short axis in the para-aortic, aortocaval, or mesenteric distributions. Prior small mesenteric lymph nodes remain stable.

Abdominal Aorta and Vessels:
The abdominal aorta is normal in caliber without aneurysmal dilation. No atherosclerotic change beyond mild wall irregularity.

Ascites and Peritoneum:
No ascites. No peritoneal nodularity or omental thickening to suggest peritoneal carcinomatosis.

---

PELVIS:

Primary Colorectal Tumor:
A heterogeneously enhancing mural mass in the sigmoid colon is again identified, now measuring approximately 3.3 cm in greatest axial dimension in the region of the sigmoid colon wall thickening, comparable to 3.2 cm on the prior study. There is persistent pericolonic fat stranding in the adjacent mesentery. No significant interval change in the primary lesion.

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
Previously identified regional lymph nodes in the sigmoid mesentery remain present. The largest node measures approximately 1.2 cm in short axis, stable compared to prior. No new pelvic sidewall, iliac, or inguinal lymphadenopathy.

Urinary Bladder:
The urinary bladder is partially distended and appears unremarkable without wall thickening or intraluminal filling defect.

Pelvic Organs (Male):
The prostate gland is mildly enlarged, consistent with benign prostatic hyperplasia. No suspicious prostatic lesions. Seminal vesicles appear symmetric.

Pelvic Soft Tissues and Osseous Structures:
No pelvic free fluid. No presacral mass. Iliac wings, sacrum, and visualized proximal femora are without suspicious lesion.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. TWO NEW PULMONARY NODULES, both located in the right lung (posterior basal segment of the right lower lobe measuring 1.1 cm and posterior segment of the right upper lobe measuring 0.7 cm), not present on the prior examination performed approximately 4 months ago. In the clinical setting of known metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma with KRAS G12D mutation, these new nodules are highly suspicious for pulmonary metastatic disease. This represents interval appearance of extrahepatic disease beyond the previously known hepatic metastases.

2. HEPATIC METASTASES — marginal interval increase in the dominant segment VI lesion (now 2.5 cm, previously 2.3 cm) and minimally increased segment VII lesion (0.5 cm from 0.4 cm), following a trend of disease that had been substantially reduced but not eliminated during induction therapy. The liver metastases, while not dramatically enlarged, demonstrate early signs of disease re-expansion after the period of near-stabilization achieved during induction FOLFOX/bevacizumab.

3. PRIMARY SIGMOID COLON MASS stable at approximately 3.3 cm.

4. NO PERITONEAL CARCINOMATOSIS, NO ASCITES identified on this examination.

5. In summary, the overall picture is one of disease progression in the context of maintenance chemotherapy, primarily driven by the development of new pulmonary metastatic lesions, with concurrent early increase in hepatic disease burden. Correlation with tumor markers (CEA, CA 19-9) and clinical assessment recommended.
